name: Stephan's Quintet
introduction: Five galaxies appear locked together in this image — but one of them is lying. Four galaxies (NGC 7317, 7318a, 7318b, and 7319) are genuinely bound in a slow-motion gravitational catastrophe 290 million light-years away, passing through each other over hundreds of millions of years, their shapes distorted by tidal forces, their gas torn into intergalactic streams. The fifth, NGC 7320, is a cosmic impostor — a foreground galaxy 40 times closer to us at just 40 million light-years, which happens to line up along the same sight line. Webb's resolution is fine enough to resolve individual star clusters inside NGC 7320 while the other four galaxies blur into their collective glow. The most dramatic feature is the enormous shockwave running between the interacting galaxies — created when NGC 7318b plunges through the group at 800 km/s, heating intergalactic gas to millions of degrees. This collision has been playing out for hundreds of millions of years and will continue for hundreds of millions more. Our Milky Way faces a similar future with the Andromeda Galaxy. Explore the hotspots to understand what a galaxy collision actually looks like from the outside.

hotspots.label: The Intergalactic Shock Wave
hotspots.description: This vast arc of glowing gas is a shockwave created when NGC 7318b punches through the group at 800 km/s, heating intergalactic gas to tens of millions of degrees. The shock front spans a region larger than our entire Milky Way. Webb's spectroscopy found that this heated gas contains complex molecular chemistry — carbon compounds forming in plasma generated by a cosmic collision. The shockwave radiates in X-rays, infrared, and radio — Webb sees the infrared glow of gas cooling after the impact. What does it mean that chemistry happens even here, in the violent space between galaxies?

hotspots.label: NGC 7320 — The Impostor
hotspots.description: This blue spiral galaxy looks like part of the group — but it's 40 million light-years away, while the others are 290 million. It shares only the line of sight, not the gravitational drama. Webb's resolution reveals individual star-forming regions and star clusters within NGC 7320 that would be far too small to resolve at the true quintet's distance. This 'sixth member' confused astronomers since the quintet's discovery in 1877 and wasn't confirmed as a foreground object until its radial velocity was measured. The universe is full of these chance alignments that fool our eyes.

hotspots.label: NGC 7318a & 7318b — The Collision
hotspots.description: Two spiral galaxies are actively passing through each other here. Their individual stars are too widely spaced to collide — but their gas clouds slam together, triggering bursts of new star formation visible as bright blue knots. Tidal forces have already stripped streamers of stars into intergalactic space. This merger will play out over hundreds of millions of years; what emerges will be a single, larger, irregular galaxy with a mixed stellar population recording every phase of the collision. Our Milky Way will undergo something similar with Andromeda in roughly 4 billion years.

hotspots.label: NGC 7319 — Active Nucleus
hotspots.description: NGC 7319 harbors a Seyfert 2 active galactic nucleus — a supermassive black hole actively consuming surrounding material. Its AGN outshines the rest of the galaxy in X-rays and releases more energy per second than a billion normal stars. Webb's spectroscopy detected the AGN's radiation signature even through the galaxy's dusty disk. Active nuclei like this were far more common in the early universe; we may be seeing NGC 7319's final energetic phase before it settles into quiescence. The gravitational stress of the ongoing group interaction may be funneling fresh material toward the black hole.

scaleTranslations: The four true members of Stephan's Quintet are 290 million light-years away — the light you're seeing left during the Carboniferous period, when Earth's first forests were growing and long before dinosaurs existed.

The intergalactic shockwave running between the galaxies is larger than our entire Milky Way — 100,000 light-years of superheated plasma generated by a collision playing out over hundreds of millions of years.
NGC 7318b is plunging through the group at 800 km/s — fast enough to travel from Earth to the Moon in under 8 minutes. One 'pass' through the cluster takes about 100 million years to complete.
